essence: Banks earn on the spread: raise money cheap (deposits), lend it dearer (loans) - net interest margin is the engine, fee income the topping.
It's a leveraged, trust-based business: a thin sliver of equity supports a balance sheet many times its size, so risk management decides who survives.
Cheap deposits - especially current/savings (CASA) - are the scarce raw material; whoever gathers low-cost, sticky deposits wins.
Heavily regulated on capital, liquidity and provisioning - the regulator (RBI) is effectively a co-pilot.
The credit cycle is everything: lend aggressively at the top and you pay for it through bad loans at the bottom.

definition.oneLiner: Institutions that take deposits and extend credit, intermediating between savers and borrowers while running the payments and savings system.
definition.jobToBeDone: Keep money safe, move it, and put it to work - letting people and businesses save, borrow, pay, and manage financial risk.

stages: Funding & treasury (manage cost of funds, liquidity)
Cross-sell & fee services (cards, wealth, insurance, payments)
valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools concentrate in low-cost-deposit franchises and high-yield retail lending (cards, personal, vehicle loans); corporate lending is thin-margin and competitive, while fee/transaction banking and wealth are prized for capital-light returns.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is migrating from balance-sheet lending -> payments & transaction banking -> embedded finance & data-driven distribution, as fintechs and UPI commoditise the rails.

variants: Universal bank (retail + corporate + treasury under one roof)
Retail-led private bank (high CASA, cards, mortgages)
Public-sector bank (scale, priority-sector mandate, government ownership)

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Net interest income per rupee of assets, plus fee income
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Assets x Net Interest Margin. NIM widens with cheaper deposits (CASA), better loan pricing, and a higher-yield retail mix; fee income adds a capital-light layer.
keyKPIs: CASA ratio - share of low-cost current/savings deposits
GNPA / NNPA - gross / net non-performing assets
Credit cost - provisions as % of loans

costStructure.shape: Two cost layers: operating costs (branches, staff, tech) and credit costs (loan-loss provisions). Operating leverage is real, but credit losses are the swing factor that can wipe out a year's profit.
majorPools: Interest expense on deposits & borrowings (cost of funds)
costStructure.leverage: A high fixed operating base means each incremental low-risk loan or deposit adds strong marginal profit - until the credit cycle turns and provisions surge.

moats: Low-cost deposit franchise (sticky CASA - the deepest moat)
competitionAndMoats.concentration: Moderately concentrated: a handful of large private banks plus dominant public-sector banks, with a long tail of regional, cooperative, small-finance and payment banks.

structuralForces.regulation: Very heavy - RBI sets capital (Basel III), liquidity (LCR), reserves (CRR/SLR), priority-sector lending and provisioning norms. Regulatory action is the single biggest swing factor.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Strongly tied to the credit and interest-rate cycles: NIMs move with rates, bad loans spike in downturns - decisions made at the top of the cycle are paid for at the bottom.
structuralForces.substitution: Fintechs, NBFCs, UPI and capital markets disintermediate parts of the chain - payments, lending and savings - pushing banks toward being regulated balance sheets behind others' front-ends.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Depositors (suppliers of funds) gain power as rates rise and switching gets easier; large corporate borrowers command fine pricing; core-banking/tech vendors hold moderate power.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium-to-high - banks are among the most active enterprise AI adopters in fraud, credit and service, though legacy cores slow deployment.
keyUseCases: Customer service chatbots & call-centre automation (large cost pool)
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: Biggest near-term value in credit decisioning (better risk pricing), operations/servicing (cost-to-serve) and fraud prevention; longer-term, AI-native distribution and hyper-personalised products reshape the front end.
opportunities: Expand credit to thin-file customers via alternative data
threatsDisruption: Fintech / AI-native lenders with lower cost structures
Model risk, bias and regulatory scrutiny of AI decisions
Erosion of the customer relationship if banks become back-end utilities

ranges: NIM: ~3-4% (Indian banks); lower for wholesale-heavy banks
CASA ratio: ~40-50% for strong retail banks
GNPA: ~2-4% post clean-up; stressed banks higher
Capital adequacy (CRAR): >11.5% regulatory minimum; banks run ~15-18%
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Lower cost of funds (grow CASA, sticky deposits)
Improve risk-adjusted lending mix (higher-yield retail, control credit cost)
Cut cost-to-income (digital, branch productivity, AI ops)
Cross-sell & deepen relationships (raise revenue per customer)

keyTerms.NIM: Net Interest Margin - the spread between interest earned on assets and paid on funds, per rupee of assets.
keyTerms.CASA: Current Account / Savings Account - low-cost deposits; a high CASA ratio means cheaper funding.
keyTerms.NPA: Non-Performing Asset - a loan where the borrower has stopped paying; high NPAs signal bad lending.
keyTerms.Provisioning: Money set aside against expected loan losses; it hits profit when credit goes bad.
keyTerms.CRAR: Capital to Risk-weighted Assets Ratio - the regulatory cushion of capital against risky assets.
keyTerms.Cost of funds: The average interest rate a bank pays to raise deposits and borrowings.
keyTerms.Cost-to-income: Operating costs as a share of income; a core efficiency measure.
keyTerms.Priority-sector lending: RBI-mandated share of lending to agriculture, MSMEs and weaker sections.
keyTerms.CRR / SLR: Cash Reserve Ratio / Statutory Liquidity Ratio - portions of deposits parked with RBI / in government securities.

questionsToAskFirst: Is the issue margin (NIM) or volume (loan/deposit growth)?
What's the CASA ratio and the trend in deposit costs?
How healthy is asset quality (GNPA, slippages, credit cost)?
Retail vs corporate mix - and how is it shifting?
Where are we in the rate and credit cycle?
Is capital adequate to fund growth?
rookieTraps: Chasing loan growth while ignoring credit quality (growth that turns into NPAs)
Reading reported profit without checking provisioning adequacy
Ignoring the rate cycle when judging NIM
Treating all deposits as equal - cost and stickiness vary hugely

whatsUniqueHere: Banking is the business of confidence and leverage: a thin sliver of equity supports a balance sheet many times its size, so trust is the product and risk management is the strategy. Money is a commodity, yet the cost and stickiness of deposits, and the discipline of underwriting, separate the great franchises from the fragile ones - and the regulator sits permanently in the room.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A three-tier system: large public-sector banks (led by SBI) still hold the biggest share; a strong cluster of private banks (HDFC Bank, ICICI, Axis, Kotak) dominates profitability; plus small-finance, payment, cooperative and regional rural banks. NBFCs and fintechs sit alongside as fast-growing challengers.
structuralChallenges: Deposit mobilisation getting harder (competition for savings)
Thin financial-inclusion economics in rural areas
Cyber & fraud risk as digital scales
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Insurance - regulated, balance-sheet, risk-priced, long-tail liabilities

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India: SBI the largest by assets; HDFC Bank the largest private bank (enlarged after the HDFC Ltd merger); ICICI, Axis, Kotak strong private players; PSU banks consolidated and much healthier after the NPA clean-up. Credit growth has run ahead of deposit growth, making low-cost deposits the key battleground.
keyTrends: Deposit war - banks competing hard for CASA & term deposits
Strong retail & unsecured credit growth (with RBI caution)
Asset quality at multi-year highs (low GNPAs)
Rapid digital / UPI adoption & fintech partnerships
RBI tightening on unsecured lending & NBFC exposures
